title: [SchedulingAnalysis] Support scheduling and pipelining full loop nests.
body:
I am opening this issue to capture an offline discussion I had a while back with @jopperm.

At the moment, we restrict our scheduling of Affine loops to only consider the innermost loop, and assume an entire iteration of the inner loop completes sequentially before the next iterations of the outer loop(s). This is enforced in a couple places:

https://github.com/llvm/circt/blob/c0eb58266c9e3f834e2af36816ef9db82958970e/lib/Analysis/DependenceAnalysis.cpp#L55-L56
https://github.com/llvm/circt/blob/c0eb58266c9e3f834e2af36816ef9db82958970e/lib/Analysis/SchedulingAnalysis.cpp#L35
https://github.com/llvm/circt/blob/c0eb58266c9e3f834e2af36816ef9db82958970e/lib/Analysis/SchedulingAnalysis.cpp#L68-L71

At a minimum we would need to relax those places, before implementing something intelligent in AffineToStaticLogic.

Here is what Julian said previously:

> I also tried to take a step back from the actual implementation, and thought about which dependences we need to model in the scheduling problem. I think it boils down to "everything that isn't otherwise ensured by a sequential execution". Looking at the upstream analysis, they don't consider the intra-iter deps because in their world, the instructions of an iteration always execute in order, which doesn't hold for our HLS pipelined datapath.
>
> So if we lower for a datapath which computes the iterations of the innermost in a pipelined fashion, but different activations of the (innermost) loop are sequentialised, it's enough to look at deps between the innermost loop's iterations.
>
> Like this:
>
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1182100/145445329-ac555a92-97d6-4cff-9e9c-97f8d9369f36.png)
>
> But we could also construct a datapath for an entire loop nest, and input a vector of iteration variables into the datapath. Then our "iterations" would be 0:<0,0>, 1: <0,1>, ..., m:<0,m-1>, m+1:<1,0>, ..., n*m:<n-1, m-1>
>
> Like this:
>
> ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1182100/145445385-8da890ca-f3d7-401d-b368-226a01a037b3.png)

It would be very cool to move from the current, first diagram, to the proposed, second diagram.

Another question is what to do when the outer loops can be parallelized, but that is a separate issue, which I think relates to the generation of systolic arrays. At any rate, supporting the tight pipelining of sequential, nested loops seems like an interesting area to explore.
